OURRENT ATTAIRS.

The hungry salary hunters of the I.A.0.S. are still
howling for a State subsidy. There are other poets in
addition to Yeats who want doles from the taxes. The
first intimation that we had in the Press that Sir
Horace was back again was a report of a whine for
doles in Belfast. He was in nice company. M.
McMordie, the Belfast Lord Mayor, pre51d$d at this

_ meeting of the Ulster Branch of the I.A.0.8., and the
_speakers included the notorious Privy Councillors,
Thomas Sinclair and the Marquis of Londonderry—
lice Irish company. Sir Horace is now about 8s sick
‘and sore as William O’Brien, and both find themselves
ked into the Carrion Crow camp. It was the same

d story, those alleged ‘100,000 organised farmers

were once more trotted out, the non-political dodge was
aired, and the incompatible cries of self-help and a
whine for a State dole were orated about jn one breath
at this meeting. The I.A.O.S. officials may keep cry-
ing for this dole, but they had better see to it that the
alleged 100,000 organised farmers subscribe enough to
pay their salaries. ‘T am not the least anxious about
this Development Grant,’’ said Sir Horace. What in
the name of common sense is all-the whining for then ?
It is rather hard to pin these gentry down, as they are
pretty slim and slippy. The division of people into
productive and non-productive classes is very artificial,
it is useful for many purposes, but it should not hide
the fact—and it often does hide it—that many of the
so-called non-producers are economically as much pro-
ducers as the man at the plough. Necessary distribu-
tors are as essential to the full process of economic
production as the men who till the land, for a thing is
not fully economically produced until it is being con-
sumed. There may be a great deal of waste and bad
management in distribution as there may be similar
waste and overlapping in State Departments, and there
is a big waste in “‘great’’ statesmen who might have
made useful cogs in the industrial machine.

Part of our own propaganda when we started was that
the mind of the country had got such a twist that it
was almost divorced from the idea of manufacturing
anything outside crude farming, and that there was an
unnatural rush to open shops, particularly publichouses.
We succeeded in a fairly short time in impressing that
view on the country, and a consequent enthusiasm for
he revival of industries was the result. There were
historical reasons that accounted, or partly accounted,
or the state into which the country had fallen, and
he exactions of the landlord class and consequent low
standard of living, particularly in some parts of the
ountry, created a state of things that induced people
o attempt to make a living out of little huxter’s shops
1at were as much uneconomic holdings as some of the
miserable bits of so-called farms from which landlords
exacted exorbitant rents and for which they are now
getting fabulous purchase terms. There were too
many huxters and too much huxtering in the country
ten years ago, but we think shopkeeping has advanced
some degrees since then, though we believe that yet
there is great need of improvement, and that there are
too many trying to eke out a living keeping unneces-
sary shops. But as the country advances its shops
and its standard of living and its business methods
must advance also. A person has a right to open a
small huxter shop even if the community is already
more than fully supplied, but in a progressing, go-
ahead country, as Ireland will be when we get Home
Rule, it will not be & question of ekeing out a miser-
able living, but of taking one’s place in vigorous com-
mercial and industrial life: Sir Horace, at Belfast,
said, speaking of the small country trader:—

We say that no non-productive class has any
right to interfere with the freedom of a productive
class to obtain everything it requires for the pur-
poses of its industry upon the most advantageous
terms.

In the first place the small country trader, in so far as
he serves o necessary purpose—and in the main he
does that—is as much a producer as the farmer, organ-
ised or unorganised. With the second part of the sen-
tence we suppose all would agree: bub surely the ob-
jection is that one section should be subsidised out of
taxes to which both sections have to pay to help it to

fight the other section. Surely this is a matter where
the business of the State is to keep the ring and help

~ neither party with public monvy.

Sir Horace wants fewer shops. He said :—
The Irish agricultural producers simply cannot
_ do their vitally important part in strengthening
the economic state of the country if they have 1ot
only to meet the competition of the world’s market,
but also to support a number of non-productive
traders out of all proportion to the needs of the
business.

N
Sir Horace wants to do away with a lot of traders. So
far as we are concerned we would like to see all unne-
cesary middlemen weeded out. But the questions are
what are unnecessary middlemen, and by what plan
or series of plans are they to be squeezed out.  And
what has this general proposition to do with State-
subsidised co-operation anyway? You cannot prevent
middlemen doing business wherever they see a chance
of making a living and you cannot well ask them to
pay part of the funds of a campaign aimed at their own
extinction. Sir Horace also said :—

1 do not suggest—I would take no part in—any-
thing like a war upon trade, but it is merely folly
to shut our eyes to the inevitable consequences of
such an economic fact as the wholly abnormal pro-
portion which distributors bear to producers in our
rural communities. The true wisdom is to build
up our chief wealth-producing industries as rapidly
as possible by a more intensive agricultural, com-
bined with a highly developed co-operative system
of business and a brighter social life. We shall
thus restore the dignity of the farmer’s calling
which it used to enjoy, and then, instead of their
sons aspiring to become shopkeepers, the sons of
the surplus traders may be induced to aspire to
the ranks of the too few agricultural producers
and town manufacturers.

That is pure unadulterated LEaDER doctrine.

Plunkett” has lost his way like Screeching William,
and we doubt if one or the other will ever find it again.
They both have a certain amount of truth in them,
but they are hopelessly muddled and compromised by
their interested backers. There has been an enormous
lot of money, subscribed money and State money, spent
already on the I.A.O.S. That society say they have
** 100,000 organised farmers.”” No one insists on these
alleged ““ 100,000”" propping up ‘‘non-productive
traders out of all proportion to the needs of the busi-
ness’’; but these alleged superfluous traders say, and
we think rightly say, ' if we are to be wiped out, let
the organised farmers do it off their own bat and nob
by the help of State subsidies comprised partly of our
own money.”’ There is competition amongst even
country traders and there is development. The village
*“ emporium’’ tends to swallow up and absorb smaller
fry in the shop line, and as it can only do so by becom-
“ing @ more economical machine of distribution than a
series of huxters the tendency is towards reducing waste

_in the distributive section of production.

Plunkett has been forced into the Carson, * ITrish
Times,”” English ‘‘Times’’ crowd and he has been made
a tool of by the hungry mandarins of the T'A.0.8.—
A, E., the escaped fairy, and the other poets, philoso-
phers and non-productive traders oub of all proportion

A correspondent sends us a page from a recent issue
of an Tnglish ha’penny illustrated paper. The page
contains a picture of a battalion of men who apparently
had been put through their facings at Portadown where
““civil and religious liberty’’ comes from. It is stated
that ‘“ on Saturday 1,000 men marched in squads to
the Market Square at Portadown, where they were re-
viewed by Colonel Fitzgerald, J.P., and Major Blacker,
J.P., of Carrickblacker. Colonel Fitzgerald 'congra'tu-
Jated the instructors and the battalion on their bearing
and appearance, and said they were drilling to show the
English and the Scotch that under no circumstances
would they ever submit to be governed by a Home Rule
Parliament. The force afterwards marched through
the principal thoroughfares of the district.” It is also
stated in the paper referred to that ‘* there is no doubt
that the members of the Unionist clubs in the North
of Treland are drilling nightly and preparing for emer-
gencies.”’ Now we all have our opinion of how much,
or how little, importance to attach to the doings of
these hypothetical rebels against the King, but cne im-
portant point is that this sort of thing goes on publicly.
In the Portadown case we see that a Colonel and a
Major—we canuot say whether they ave real soldiers or
militia men—and both J.P.’s, were openly connected
with it. TIn view of the fact that these hypothetical
traitors to the King and Constitution are performing in
the light of day, we think it would be well for Young
Treland to pour into the ranks of the national youths'
brigades. The boy of fifteen will in three years be the
potential young fighting man of eighteen—a good fight-
ing age. We may laugh at the Portadown parade, but

as this thing is going on quite openly we would be fool- *

ish not to do a bit of drilling on our side.

Carna, in the Co. Galway, is one of the most Trish-
speaking districts in Treland. - There was a * ball
there recently, got up, we suppose, by the local “‘elite.””
We have received a clipping from' a local paper describ-
ing the event. We are told that *‘ the wmsthetic faculty
of the ladies who decorated the ballroom shows clearly
that even in romantic Carna there are refined tastes.”
That is rather comically put certainly. We are told
also that the M.C. ‘‘ certainly spared no pains to carry
out the business of the ball in all its majestic decorum.’”
Apparently the English of Carna is a bit erude. * There
was no professional musician but there were plenty of
willing amateurs to render every opportunity to those
who wished to participate in the light fantastic toe.””

With reference to Trish in Belfast University; a cor-
respondent writes :—'* Your Belfast correspondent in
lovalty to Mr. Burke of that city rather allows lis
friendly zeal to carry him away. No attack was made

on the Senator; but, surely, one is at liberty to make a
r query on a matter appearing in the Press.  The mea-
greness of the reported matter should be found fault
with and not the natural question which arises there-
from.  The ‘Independent’ states that ‘the Senate
of the Belfast University, whilst sympathi sing with
the request of the Gaelic League for the establishment
of a Chair, instead of Lectureship in Irish, regrets that
scareity of funds does not permit of this being done at
present, but promises to considei the matter favour-
ably when the question of increase of salaries comes
up. The resolution was passed, Mr. Burke dissent-
ing." Now, if words convey anything, this report would
lead ono to believe that Mr. Burke was opposed to the
creation of an Irish Chair. Happily we ave shown that
this is not the case, and at the same time we are told
© that there are many matters which require the atten-
tion of the Press being drawn on behalf of the Catholic
body.” Why, in the name of common sense, are these
same matters not sent to the LEaper? In Galway,
not long since, the University got rather a nasty drub-
bing from our plucky Dublin weekly. By all means,
then, let the Catholics of Treland judge of the Belfast
patriots.””

The shrieking sisterhood provide us with some mate-
rial for *‘ copy,” for which we have to specially thank
themn at this particular time with a bank holiday inter-
rupting the even tenor of our routine. The dear girls
are very amusing—to read about. Their meeting last
week was particularly spicy, and ‘‘the dastardly trick-
ery of the Irish Nationalist Party '’ got it hot from the

2 Skeffy gang. A great Irishman, of whom we do not
. remember to have heard tell before, Rev. L. Saville
bt Hicks, M.A., Unitarian Minister, delivered an addvess

*the Suffers broke out into hip, hip, hurrahs!

- We at once scented comedy when we read that Mrs.

‘scented was nof of such a nature that we could reason-

- ably expect the Professor to enjoy it. Mrs. Oldham
- remarked that “ she personally was not a born Trish--

es Sir !!!

Mrs. Skeflington got up to announce the future
policy of the League. ‘' As far as the Irish Women’s
Franchise League were concerned, they were done with
the Irish Party (prolonged cheers).” ~ Even poor Mr.
David Sheehy, M.P.; is disowned. ‘‘ She felt that
she was a better Irish Nationalist than any Irish mem-
ber (applause).’” Then came the world-quaking infor-
mation that she had sent in her resignation to the
Yibs.  *“She advised all Irishwomen to follow her
example, and to leave any organisation connected offi-
cially with the Irish Party as a protest.”” There was
applause at that, in which, no doubt, all the anti-Irish
females—and we suppose there were some present—
Jjoined heartily.

They are done with the Irish Party, but said she :—
** There still remained, forbunately for them, the Irish
people "—If the Irish people could get at them | They
are to ask the Convention to receive a deputation :
indeed they may ask, but they will be wasting their
energies. The discovery in London of the letter of
Mrs. Cousins already had let the cat out of the bag
concerning their Convention prospects. The deputation
 must be prepared at all costs’’ to approach the
National Convention, and she-called upon their male
sympathisers to help them. *‘‘Kelly and Burke and
Shea’’ won’t be in it with Skeffy and Kettle and Cruso
on that great day! And we suppose Charles Hubert
Oldham will have to lend a hand too. The policy of
the Suffers is one of ‘‘ war against the members of the
Irish Party.”” Here is a prospect to gladden: the hearts
of the anti-Home Rulers— She thought she might
say that a new phase of militancy would be entered
upon if the Convention finally denied them their right
to representation under the Home Rule Bill.”” There’s
a pretty picture for you—a few female fanatics, foreign
or home-bred as they may be, and their poor hen-
pecked husbands holding up the National Convention!

Of course the ‘‘ Irish Times’” cannot conceal its
pleasure at what it hopes may be a new obstacle in the
way of our progress towards Home Rule, but it is afraid
of *“ militant” tactics.  Such tactics would not suit
its book.

The anti-Home Rulers in Ireland have such a weak
case that the *‘ No Popery’’ and anti-Catholic cries are
used to such an extent that even some of the “ thirty
shilling dirties '’ who defame: their country in England
must be getting ashamed of the game. It would be a
great relief to the Carrion Crows if the Suffers could
create a little diversion in their favour.

" The Suffers in England have a lot of money col-
lected: we would like to know how much, if any, of
that money is lavished on the gang here whose‘pollcx
now is ‘‘ war against the members of the Irish Party.’

* Sazanc’” writes :—Another book into which I was
looking recently is the Rev. Stephen Brown’s ‘ Guide
to Irish Books.” I came upon a good specimen
of loose, slobbery statement which purports to
give accurate information.  The little series of Irish
song books brought out by.an tata Paopurs Dpeatnae
contains, according to the writer, about a dozen songs
in each 1d. number. ~Well, the expression ‘ about a
dozen’ can only mean anything between 10 and 14,
according to recognised usage of speech. The true
facts are these: “ puinn na pmot,” No. 1, contains 21
songs ; Nos. 2 and 38 contain 20 each ; No. 4,“Cnuapacc”
unrevised, contains 15, with pocton ; Nos. 5 and 6
contain 16 and 17 respectively. = A book which purports
to give up-to-date, accurate information should not be
discovered tripping.  Inaccuracy in small matters is
calculated to make readers suspicious of the informa-
tion contained in the whole.”’

34 There was a ** show of some of the work done by the
T students of the Technical School "’ in Birr on Wednes-

T day, 3rd April. It was interesting from many points
n ! of view. Last May the present Principal, Mr. D. A.

v Muleahy, was appointed after an extraordinary contest.
). Applications from candidates—both IEnglish and Scotch

—who were not qualified at all according to the terms
of the advertisement—were very favourably received
by a minority of the:committee and by the Department.
Mr. Muleahy, who has been working in Birr 6 years
and was fully qualified, was not considered eligible by
the Department, and was bitterly opposed by the landed
gentry and by some of the shopkeepers on the commit-
tee, whose business prosperity depends in some degree
on the aforementioned landed gentry. The appointment
was made. The Department refused to sanction him.
The chairman, the Rev. Fr. O’Reilly, of Kilcormac, re-
fused to call & special meeting or to reconsider the de-
{ cision of the committee, the school term was due to
begin, and so the Department provisionally recognised
Mr. Mulcahy for that term. The committee of King's
Co. gave a much-needed example to committees in Tre-
land, and now that the winter session is over it is inte-
resting to see the result.

The number of students attending the classes was
about twice as great as in former years, and, a much
more important consideration, the average attendance
. of each student was much higher; but the actual work
done by the students is to the ordinary observer the
most reliable test of the worth of a manual instructor
or any technical teacher. There were different sections
of work, representing the subjects of the various mem-
bers of the teaching staff, Mr. Mulcahy’s own display
being by far the largest and most interesting. ~ Wood
worlk, from little key racks and plain brackets to beau-
tifully inlaid cabinets, carved stools, copies of ancient
Irish furniture, and a_very varied collection of what
one might call intermediate’articles. The beauty of the
collection was, we are told, its originality. Nothing was
duplicated. Every student had apparently worked out
an idea as it had appealed to himself, and though under
the guidance of the master, utterly independent of every
other fellow in the school. ’

One who was there writes:—*‘ I have never before
| seen a school of many students so utterly free of the
{ ¢ copy-book’ taint. It was a joy to ‘bel}o[(l the keen

appreciation of Celtic ornament and Celtic design, and

- finer still the answer of a student when u§ked did they
support Irish manufacture—‘ We are Trish mun’ufuc-
turers. All our raw material is Trish, and we don’t de-
grade it.” Behold the spirit of the technical sehool of

R. GILL admonished his hearers, in the lecture to
which your contributor, ** Setanta,”* refers in
the LEADER of the 80th ult., that the time was ripe for
them to be ‘‘ thinking, not loosely or in a piecemeal
mauner, about the industry of agriculture.”  And he
later on said he spoke ‘ plainly for the sake of getting
our ideas clear.”” ‘‘ Setanta’ does not seem to have
this advice before his mind when he adopts Mr. Gill’s
description of the occupying owners as ‘‘ National
Trustees’ in one paragraph and describes them in an-
other as “‘ the newly-established owners.” My quo-
tation does not give his exact words, but he won’t quar-
rel with me for that, as ““ ownership’’ of course implies
that owners exist. The question I wish to raise is, and
I ask ‘‘ Setanta,” or some other of your able contri-
butors, to answer, ‘‘ are the ‘ occupying owners,’ viz.,
those who have bought out the landlord’s interest in
their holdings, and who are bound to repay the loan by
annual instalments extending over a long period of
time, during this time or at the end of it when the last
instalment is paid, to consider themselves owners or
merely trustees ?”” When I speak of owners and trus-
tees I use these words in their ordinary seuse and as
commonly understood. Mr. Gill speaks of irresponsible
ownership, and “* Setanta’’ adopts his word I ask
did such a thing ever exist in any nation or community
of men ? One may as well speak of absolute or irrespon-
sible liberty as of irresponsible ownership. And if it
never existed how could it be abolished, as Mr. Gill
states? The truth seems to be that My. Gill did not
himself think clearly before he used those specious and
plausible words which may conceal the germs of anarchy
or socialism, or may mean only Mr. Gill’s winning way
of gaining popularity for the Department. Tt is true
the farmers owe a duty to the community and to them-
selves, but not on the grounds claimed by Mr. Gill, as
can be easily shown if it is required to do so. They
also owe a duty to themselves—or rather to their fami-
lies and the families of those concerned as well as they
are in the one great productive industry of this coun
and in discharging thjs duty to the best of their abi
with the aid of instruction in the best methods, th
will be performing all that human or divine law imposes
on them.

The shopkeeper who vends manure and supplies the
other requisites of the farm, as well as caters for the
domestic wants of our families, owes a duty to us, and
so does the labourer and the doctor and the clergyman,
and T hear nothing about how they discharge their
various duties.  Not a word, although in the final
analysis it can be shown that all are dependent on the
produce of the land. At least eighty per cent. of the
national wealth is directly derived from it, and what-
ever prosperity the nation attains will inevitably be in
proportion to the prosperity of the farmers of Ireland.
This was clearly seen when the Land War began and

l M glad that my notice of Wilfrid Ward’s life of

Newman has led to the article by “ Y.”” I agree
with the writer that ‘* history must be as complete as
is possible if we ave to trust to it for real knowledge."
He tells us that Dr. Cullen had an immense correspon-

- dence, some of which he has seen; and 1 quite agree

with him that we should have Dr. Cullen’s side of the
early history of the Catholic University. To know the
history of those who have gone before us, is a very
useful education, but it must be the real history—mot
mere names and dates of great events. The Very Rev.
Monsignor Ward has written a real history o the
Catholic Church in England during the first years of
the last century. I wish some Irish priest would write
the real history of the Church in Ireland, giving us the
actual letters of the Irish Churchmen in the past. Car-
dinal Moran gave us such a history of the times of the
Venerable Oliver Plunkett, Archbishop of Armagh. A
life of Cardinal Cullen would be very useful, but do not
leb it be a false history, giving only one side, but let
it give the whole truth. T.eo XIII. told Catholic his-
torians not to be afraid of the truth. God’s Church
does not suffer from the truth being told.

bishop of Dublin, when I was a very young child. I
can remember his confirming me fifty-five years ago.
1 can remember being wearied by his numerous long
pastorals, and hearing those older than myself saying

put up a billiard table in his house in' Harcourt Street,
where he lodged seven or eight young men. One of the
Irish bishops, not Dr. Cullen, ob]ected,' on the ground
that it was encouraging gambling. His reply was:—
“Your Grace,—If I have not the billiard table, they
will go to the publichouses to play billiards. I quite
agree with “Y.,” let us have Cullen’s view of the
Catholic University, but he does not give it to us in his

for the cause (a Catholic University); the men engaged
are for me only instruments.” Alas! A Catholic Uni-
versity seems to be now perfectly impossible in Ire-
land, ‘and its old seat has been taken possession of by

CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We have received an excellently turned out volume,
‘‘ Historical Ballad Poetry of Ireland.”” The arrange-
ment is chronological, the book being divided into five
historical periods. The periods are: (1) from the com-
ing of the Milesians to Ruaidhu O’Conor, 1166 ; (2) from
O’Conor to Gerald, Earl of Kildare, 1166 to 1478; (3)
from Kildare to Flight of the Earls, 1478 to 1607; (4)
from Tlight of the Earls to Rebellion of Prince Charles
Bdward, 1607 to 1745; (5) from 1745 to Modern Times.
T'he volume is nicely bound in green cloth, and runs to
256 pages. The price is 3s. 6d., and the publishers,
The Educational Company of Ireland, 89 Talbot Street,
Dublin. We have also received from the same pub-
lishers a neat threepenny booklet of 80 pages of ‘‘Scenes
from Knocknagow.’’ This book at such a price should
receive a hearty welcome from young Ireland.

Who owns, edits or conducts the ‘“ General Adver-
tiser,”” that is put free, gratis and for nothing into our
letter box occasionally, if not weekly ? We understand
that there is some statutory regulation that certain
legal advertisements must be inserted in it, and no
doubt it lives well on that. There does not seem to be
much original matter in the advertising sheet : but even
when it clips its stuff from other papers why should it
go in for propagating blatant atheism? Under the
heading ‘‘ The Origin of Life,”” it clips about half a
column of pseudo-scientific sectarianism, We read in
this advertising sheet that *‘ the theory of creation has
iong since being relegated to the limbo of superstition.’’
Of course that is a lie, but that sort of stuff is scarcely
the kind of matter that one would expect to read in a
‘* protected "’ advertisement sheet. Another lie is
contained in the statement that * It is a fact that biolo-
gists as a class are agnostics on the subject of life-
origin.””
